name: Two Bays Greenway and Barbate Reservoir Loop
name_original: Corredor Verde Dos Bahías hasta el Embalse de Barbate – circular desde Medina Sidonia
description: Cycle a difficult 42.4-mile route from Medina Sidonia to the Barbate Reservoir, exploring diverse landscapes and historic sites.
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Medina-Sidonia traverse a landscape characterized by rolling hills, picturesque olive groves, and tranquil countryside dotted with rural farms. The region features the dense cork oak forests of Los Alcornocales Natural Park, along with meandering streams and valleys. Medina-Sidonia itself is perched on a hilltop, offering expansive views of the surrounding area. Reservoirs such as Barbate, Charco Redondo, and Almodóvar also contribute to the varied natural scenery. Best touring cycling routes around Medina-Sidonia The most popular touring cycling route is Two Bays Greenway and Barbate Reservoir Loop, a 42.4 miles (68.2 km) trail that takes 5 hours 4 minutes to complete. This route combines diverse landscapes with historical sites. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Dirt path near Medina-Sidonia – Miralamar Viewpoint loop from Medina Sidonia, a difficult 31.9 miles (51.3 km) path. This route explores dirt paths near the Miralamar Viewpoint. Local touring cyclists also love the Little climb by the Salmeta Stream – Parque de las Cañadas loop from Recinto Ferial de Medina Sidonia, a 38.7 miles (62.2 km) trail leading through the Salmeta Stream and Parque de las Cañadas, often completed in about 5 hours 13 minutes. Touring cycling around Medina-Sidonia is defined by rolling hills, olive groves, and cork oak forests, with options available for different ability levels, from leisurely rides to challenging excursions. The routes in Medina-Sidonia are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.3 stars from more than 5 reviews. More than 250 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Medina-Sidonia's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Medina-Sidonia?
A: There are over 35 touring cycling routes around Medina-Sidonia, catering to various fitness levels. You'll find a mix of easy, moderate, and challenging excursions, with the majority being more demanding rides.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see while cycling in Medina-Sidonia?
A: The region offers diverse landscapes, including rolling hills, picturesque olive groves, and tranquil countryside dotted with rural farms and charming white villages. You can also explore the dense cork oak forests of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7342563" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Alcornocales Natural Park&lt;/a&gt;, meandering streams, and valleys. The town itself is perched on a hilltop, providing breathtaking panoramic views that extend across the surrounding countryside and even reach the Bay of Cádiz.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Medina-Sidonia?
A: The mild Mediterranean climate makes spring and autumn ideal for touring cycling in Medina-Sidonia. These seasons offer pleasant temperatures, allowing riders to comfortably enjoy the scenic vistas and immerse themselves in the local Andalusian culture.

Q: Are there any family-friendly cycling routes in the area?
A: While many routes are challenging, there is at least one easy touring cycling route available. For families, consider exploring flatter sections or shorter loops that allow for a more relaxed experience amidst the tranquil countryside.

Q: What are some notable natural attractions or viewpoints along the cycling routes?
A: Cyclists can enjoy views of reservoirs such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5343436" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Charco Redondo Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3036271" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almodóvar Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;, both located within Los Alcornocales Natural Park. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7342563" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the lake's rear section in Los Alcornocales Natural Park&lt;/a&gt; is another highlight. The town's hilltop position also offers expansive panoramic views of the surrounding landscape.

Q: Are there any historical sites or landmarks I can visit while cycling?
A: Medina-Sidonia is rich in history. While cycling, you might pass by or be near historical sites. The town itself features the ancient Castle of Medina Sidonia, the beautifully preserved Moorish gate &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4118830" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arco de la Pastora&lt;/a&gt;, Roman archaeological ruins, and the impressive 16th-century Iglesia Mayor de Santa María la Coronada. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/232987" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Green Corridor of the Two Bays&lt;/a&gt; also combines diverse landscapes with historical sites.

Q: What is the general difficulty level of touring cycling routes in Medina-Sidonia?
A: The touring cycling routes around Medina-Sidonia are predominantly challenging, with 22 out of 37 routes rated as difficult. There are also 14 moderate routes and 1 easy route, offering options for different skill levels, but many routes involve significant elevation gains.

Q: Can you recommend a challenging long-distance loop route?
A: For a challenging long-distance loop, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/33902005" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Montera del Torero – Celemín Footbridge loop from el Puerto del Hombre&lt;/a&gt;. This difficult route covers 76.5 km and typically takes around 6 hours and 30 minutes to complete, featuring substantial elevation changes.

Q: What do other cyclists say about touring cycling in Medina-Sidonia?
A: The touring cycling routes in Medina-Sidonia are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.3 stars from more than 5 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, the scenic beauty of the rolling hills and cork oak forests, and the immersive experience in local Andalusian culture.

Q: Are there any routes that explore dirt paths or specific natural areas?
A: Yes, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31593597" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Dirt path near Medina-Sidonia – Miralamar Viewpoint loop from Medina Sidonia&lt;/a&gt; specifically explore dirt paths near the Miralamar Viewpoint. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31602626" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Little climb by the Salmeta Stream – Parque de las Cañadas loop from Recinto Ferial de Medina Sidonia&lt;/a&gt;, which leads through the Salmeta Stream and Parque de las Cañadas.

Q: Are there any routes that combine diverse landscapes with historical sites?
A: The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e957880032/two-bays-greenway-and-barbate-reservoir-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Two Bays Greenway and Barbate Reservoir Loop&lt;/a&gt; is an excellent choice, as it combines diverse landscapes with historical sites, offering a comprehensive touring cycling experience over 68.2 km.
tips: Section of the "Corredor verde de las dos Bahías", here the piece between Benalup and Medina Sidonia. It is one of the few designated cycling and hiking trails in the area. Wonderful to drive with MTB or the Crosser (road bike is not: good gravel road). Nice environment. Hilly, without alpine challenges. The whole way stretches over almost 100km. More here: https://es.wikipedia.org/wiki/Corredor_verde_de_las_dos_Bah%C3%ADas
Access is very easy, and we can refresh ourselves with the watering of the oleanders that is usually activated at noon.
Ascent to Medina Sidonia from Paterna of almost 3 km with a gradient of up to 13%

Remains of 3 fortifications: one Roman, one Moorish and one medieval. Even those less interested in archaeology should not miss the Castillo on their visit to Medina Sidonia, as they can enjoy a fantastic view from there.

faqs.question: How long does it take to cycle the Corredor Verde Dos Bahías to the Barbate Reservoir – circular from Medina Sidonia?
faqs.answer: This challenging touring bicycle route typically takes around 5 hours to complete. It covers approximately 68.2 kilometers with significant elevation changes, so your actual time may vary depending on your fitness level and how often you stop to enjoy the scenery.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is classified as difficult and features varied terrain. You'll encounter unpaved sections, cattle routes ("vías pecuarias"), and former roads. Some parts can be muddy, especially after rain, particularly when crossing streams. Expect demanding gradients and "potent ramps" along the way. It's best suited for mountain bikes (MTB) or cross bikes.

faqs.question: Is this trail suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is classified as 'difficult' for touring cycling due to its length (68.2 km), significant elevation gain (671 m), and varied, sometimes unpaved, terrain. It requires a good level of physical fitness and is not recommended for beginners.

faqs.question: What can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: The route offers diverse landscapes and points of interest. You'll pass through rural areas, agricultural fields, and scrubland. Key natural features include the Barbate River and its tributaries, and views of the Barbate Dam (Embalse del Celemín). You'll also experience sections of the Green Corridor of the Two Bays, and near the start/end, the historic town of Medina Sidonia with its Castle of Medina Sidonia and Plaza de España.

faqs.question: Where does the trail start and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: The circular route starts and ends in Medina Sidonia. While specific trailhead parking isn't detailed, Medina Sidonia is a town, so you should be able to find parking within the town itself, particularly near the historic center, to begin your ride.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: To avoid extreme heat and potential muddy conditions, the best times to cycle this route are generally spring and autumn. Summers can be very hot in Andalusia, and heavy winter rains can make some unpaved sections particularly challenging and muddy.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route utilizes and intersects with several traditional pathways. You'll ride along sections of the Corredor Verde Dos Bahías, Corredor Verde de las Dos Bahías, Camino de Santiago del Estrecho, and the Cañada Real de Algeciras Tarifa y Medina Sidonia.

faqs.question: Is the Corredor Verde Dos Bahías to the Barbate Reservoir – circular from Medina Sidonia dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route passes through rural areas and natural landscapes, specific regulations for dogs on this particular circular route are not detailed. However, as a general rule for green corridors and natural parks in Spain, dogs are usually permitted but must be kept on a leash, especially to protect wildlife and livestock. Please ensure you clean up after your pet.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for this route or the Parque Natural Los Alcornocales?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need a permit or to pay an entrance fee to cycle this route. A significant portion of the broader Corredor Verde traverses the Parque Natural de Los Alcornocales, but access to the park itself for activities like cycling on designated routes is generally free and does not require a permit.

faqs.question: What type of bicycle is recommended for this route?
faqs.answer: Given the 'difficult' classification, varied terrain, and unpaved sections, a mountain bike (MTB) or a cross bike is highly recommended. Road bikes are not suitable for this route due to the challenging surfaces and potential for mud.

faqs.question: Are there any facilities like cafes or water stops along the way?
faqs.answer: The route primarily goes through rural areas, so dedicated cafes or water stops directly on the trail are limited. It's advisable to carry sufficient water and snacks for the entire 5-hour journey. Medina Sidonia, your starting and ending point, offers various amenities, including cafes and restaurants.

waypoints.name: Green Corridor of the Two Bays
